text: Here are three ways of doing a laparoscopic Pringle maneuver.

The surgeon creates a window in the pars flaccida and introduces a disposable plastic trocar in the left upper abdomen. Umbilical tape is threaded through the window and then through the foramen of Winslow.
The surgeon aligns the marked umbilical tape at its center point and threads its ends through a disposable plastic trocar, ensuring the soft edges of the trocar do not traumatize the porta hepatis.

During the right hepatectomy, bleeding is observed from a portal branch. The surgeon deploys the Pringle maneuver by advancing the trocar over the umbilical tape and tightening a clamp externally, achieving good hemostasis to allow the resection to proceed.
A trocar and grasper are introduced through a port high in the right upper abdomen. The grasper is used to pass the umbilical tape in the opposite direction through the foramen of Winslow.
The umbilical tape, marked at its midpoint, is positioned appropriately. The ends are grasped and brought through the plastic disposable trocar, preparing for the Pringle maneuver if needed.
The surgeon places a Rommel tourniquet laparoscopically, carefully deploying an umbilical tape through the surgical field.

The surgeon brings the ends of the umbilical tape out through a lower abdominal incision and loads them onto a red rubber catheter. A clamp is placed externally on the catheter, which can be advanced if needed, similar to a Rommel tourniquet in open surgery. The red rubber catheter is positioned in the surgical field.
